claim: Antibiotics cure the common cold and flu
correction: The common cold and flu are caused by viruses. Antibiotics only work against bacterial infections and have no effect on viral illnesses.

claim: You should stop taking antibiotics once you feel better
correction: Always complete the full prescribed course. Stopping early can leave resistant bacteria alive, which may multiply and spread.

claim: Antibiotic resistance means your body becomes resistant to antibiotics
correction: It's the bacteria that become resistant, not your body. Resistant bacteria can spread to other people, making infections harder to treat for everyone.

claim: Antimicrobial resistance is a growing global health threat
correction: AMR is recognized by WHO as one of the top 10 global public health threats facing humanity. Without action, common infections could become untreatable.

claim: You can use leftover antibiotics from a previous illness
correction: Different infections require different antibiotics and dosages. Using leftover medicine may be the wrong drug, wrong dose, or an expired product, all of which can promote resistance.

claim: Antibiotics have side effects only when misused
correction: All antibiotics can cause side effects (diarrhea, allergic reactions, etc.) even when used correctly. This is another reason they should only be taken when truly necessary.

claim: Hand washing helps fight antibiotic resistance
correction: Good hand hygiene prevents infections from spreading, reducing the overall need for antibiotics and thus slowing resistance development.

claim: Antibiotic resistance only affects people who take antibiotics frequently
correction: Anyone can get a resistant infection, even those who have never taken antibiotics. Resistant bacteria can spread through communities, food, water, and the environment.

claim: Stronger antibiotics are always more effective
correction: The 'right' antibiotic depends on the specific bacteria causing the infection. A narrow-spectrum antibiotic matched to the pathogen is often more effective and safer than a broad-spectrum one.

claim: New antibiotics will solve the resistance problem
correction: Very few new antibiotics are in development, and bacteria can develop resistance to new drugs quickly. Prevention and appropriate use of existing antibiotics are our best defense.

claim: AMR is a problem for the future, not right now
correction: AMR is already here. In 2019, nearly 1.3 million deaths were directly attributable to drug-resistant bacterial infections worldwide.
The vast majority of sore throats are caused by viruses. Only a small percentage are bacterial (like strep throat) which can be confirmed with a test.

claim: You can safely share antibiotics with family members
correction: Never share antibiotics. The drug prescribed for you may not be effective for someone else's infection and could cause harmful side effects or drive resistance.
